title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
This is a hands on exercise 1 exploration.
pacman::p_load(tidyverse)
exam_data <- read_csv("../data/Exam_data.csv")

Rows: 322 Columns: 7
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (4): ID, CLASS, GENDER, RACE
dbl (3): ENGLISH, MATHS, SCIENCE

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
Histogram of Exam Data
First, we compare exam data using a histogram

hist(exam_data$MATHS)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
ggplot objects

ggplot(data=exam_data)








element into the plot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS))









geometric objects:
geom_bar

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()








geom_dotplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)

Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with
`binwidth`.









geom_histogram

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()       

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value with `binwidth`.









we can modify the geometric object by changing geom()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="light blue")  








modify by changing aes()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")








geom_density()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()








plotting 2 kernel density lines by using color

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()








geom_boxplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot()            








notched plot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)








geom_violin()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_violin()








geom_point()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()            








combination of geom objects

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +                    
  geom_point(position="jitter", 
             size = 0.5)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
statistic function

stat()
stat_summary()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",       
               fun = "mean",         
               colour ="red",        
               size=4)               








combine geom() and stat()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun="mean",           
             colour="red",          
             size=4)          








how to add best fit curve on a scatterplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              linewidth=0.5)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'











facets
facet_wrap()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_wrap(~ CLASS)








facet_grid()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_grid(~ CLASS)










coordinates
vertical coordinate

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()








using coord_flip()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()








changing the x and y axis range

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5)

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.


`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'










ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'











themes
bar chart using gray color

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_gray()








using theme_classic()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_classic()








using theme_minimal()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

title: Home
text: This website is my coursework portfolio for ISSS608: Visual Analytics and Applications. It showcases my learning journey through Hands‑on Exercises, In‑class Exercises, and Take‑home Exercises.

title: Home
text: This website is my coursework portfolio for ISSS608: Visual Analytics and Applications. It showcases my learning journey through Hands‑on Exercises, In‑class Exercises, and Take‑home Exercises.

title: Home
text:
Motivation
To me, data tells a story, I believe that data becomes meaningful when it is visualized effectively. Through this course, I aim to strengthen my ability to transform raw data into clear, insightful visuals that support better decision‑making.

title: Home
text:
Coursework Overview

Hands‑on Exercises: Step‑by‑step practice sessions to apply new concepts done before every week’s lecture
In‑class Exercises: Activities done during weekly lecture to reinforce learning.

Take‑home Exercises:

title: Home
text:
Looking Ahead
By the end of this course, I hope to build a strong foundation in visual analytics and apply these skills to real‑world datasets. This portfolio will serve as both a record of my progress and a resource for future projects.

title: Hands-On Exercise2
text:
This is a hands on exercise 2 exploration.
In this exercise, ggrepel, ggthemes, hrbrthemes and patchwork will be used for exploration.
pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse)
We will import the data now
exam_data <- read_csv("../data/Exam_data.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

title: Hands-On Exercise2
text:
ggrepel
This pacakage is an extension of ggplot2 which provides geoms for ggplot2 to repel overlapping text.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), 
             hjust = .5, 
             vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.


`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









Here’s another example of using ggrepel

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'


Warning: ggrepel: 317 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider
increasing max.overlaps

title: Hands-On Exercise2
text:
ggthemes
ggplot2 comes with eight built-in themes. The themes are: theme_gray(), theme_bw(), theme_classic(), theme_dark(), theme_light(), theme_linedraw(), theme_minimal(), and theme_void().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") 









ggtheme package: The Economist theme

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()










ggtheme package: hrbthems
This package provides a base theme that focuses on typographic elements, including where various labels are placed as well as the fonts that are used.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()








Utilizing the elements of axis_title_size, base_size and grid to modify the above plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")

title: Hands-On Exercise2
text:
Creating Composite Graphics: pathwork methods
While several ggplot2 extensions—such as gridExtra::grid.arrange() and cowplot::plot_grid()—allow you to combine multiple graphs, the patchwork package offers an intuitive approach. Specifically built for the ggplot2 ecosystem, patchwork simplifies the creation of complex layouts using a syntax that feels like writing a simple equation. General syntax of patchwork are: The Plus Sign (+): Used to place plots side-by-side in a two-column layout. Parentheses (): Used to group subplots together, allowing you to treat multiple graphs as a single unit within a larger layout. The Division Sign (/): Used to stack plots vertically in a two-row layout.

Combining two ggplot2 graphs
First, we create each single graph naming it p1, p2, p3

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")
p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")
p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

After creating the graphs, we can utilize th simple patchwork syntax to create a composite of two histograms

p1 + p2










Combining three ggplot2 graphs
We aren’t just limited to two grids, we can plot more complex composite using the below syntax: The Slash Sign (/): Places plots on top of one another (vertical stacking) The Horizontal Slash (|): Places plots side-by-side (horizontal stacking) The Parentheses (()): Acts just like the normal parentheses, dictating the order of operations so we can nest complex layouts within a single structure.

(p1 / p2) | p3

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'











Creating a composite figure with tag
Patchwork also provides an auto-tagging function to identify subplots in text.

((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'











Creating figure with insert
Patchwork offers the inset_element() function.We can define the exact boundaries of your inset using a coordinate system from 0 to 1.

p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'











Creating a composite figure by using patchwork and ggtheme
We can combine ggthemes and patchwork. Here’s an example of a patchwork function and theme_economist()

patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
